body.text: A variable is a named container for a value. The program can read it, change it, and use it later. Without variables, every action starts from zero — the program has no memory.

body.desc: Create a variable named 'count' and store the value 0 inside it
Take what is in 'count', add 1, and store the result back

body.label: Key rule
body.text: Reassigning a variable replaces the old value entirely. It does not add a second value alongside the first.

body.desc: A string is text. Any time you want a variable to hold words instead of numbers, you wrap the value in quotes.

lines: // Creating variables. The first time you use a name, you need 'let' to declare it.

exercises.title: Predict the value
tasks: On paper first — no editor yet. Work through this line by line: let score = 10 → score = score + 5 → score = score * 2 → score = score - 4
Write down the value of score after each line.
Now open the editor. Type each line and use console.log(score) after each one to check your prediction.
Where did your prediction go wrong, if anywhere? Discuss.
exercises.hint: Work left to right. After line 1 score is 10. After line 2 it is 15. Keep going.

checks.name: score
checks.label: score ends up as 26 after all four operations

exercises.title: Write three variables
tasks: Open a fresh editor. Write three variables: your name, your age, and your favorite number.
Write a fourth variable called message that joins all three into a sentence.
Now change your age variable to a different number and run it again.
exercises.hint: Join strings and numbers with +. Example: 'I am ' + age + ' years old.'

checks.label: something is logged to the console

challenges.title: Why is the total always 0?
challenges.description: This code should add price to total, but total always prints 0. Find and fix the bug.
challenges.hint: JavaScript is case-sensitive. Is the variable name spelled exactly the same every time?
challenges.explanation: JavaScript treats 'total' and 'Total' as two completely different names. Line 3 creates a brand new variable called Total and never touches the original total — so total stays 0. Fix: change Total to total on line 3.

challenges.title: The message prints 'Hello undefined' — why?
challenges.description: Sam should be greeted by name, but something is undefined. Find and fix it.
challenges.hint: Look very carefully at how firstName is spelled on line 1 vs line 2.
challenges.explanation: The variable was declared as firstName (capital N) but used as firstname (lowercase). JavaScript can't find 'firstname', so it uses undefined instead. Fix: change firstname to firstName on line 2.

challenges.title: Score should be 30, but it prints 15
challenges.description: Something is wrong with how score is being set up. The final answer should be 30.
challenges.hint: Think about what 'let' does. You only need it once per variable.
challenges.explanation: Using 'let' twice on the same variable is an error. 'let' means create new — but score already exists. Line 2 should just be: score = 5; (no let). The rule: use let once, the first time. After that, just use the name.
